responses.questionText: A government wishes to keep the price of an essential food, such as rice, affordable to help low-income households. Assess whether a policy of fixing the maximum price for an essential food is likely to be more effective than a policy of making transfer payments to low-income households.
responses.markScheme:
Table A: AO1 Knowledge and understanding and AO2 Analysis
Level 3 (6-8 Marks): A detailed knowledge and understanding of relevant economic concepts is included, using relevant explanations. Explanations are supported by examples, where appropriate. The response clearly addresses the requirements of the question and explains economic issues, and fully develops these explanations. Analysis is developed and detailed and makes accurate and relevant use of economic concepts and theories.
Level 2 (3-5 Marks): Knowledge and understanding of some relevant economic concepts is included, using explanations and examples that are limited, over-generalised or contain inaccuracies. The response addresses the general theme of the question and the relevant economic issues, with limited development. Analysis is generally accurate with some development but little detail.
Level 1 (1-2 Marks): A small number of relevant knowledge points are included and the response is limited by significant errors or omissions. The response has little relevance to the question. Analysis where provided is largely descriptive.

Table B: AO3 Evaluation
Level 2 (3-4 Marks): Provides a justified conclusion or judgement that addresses the specific requirements of the question. Makes developed, reasoned and well-supported evaluative comment(s).
Level 1 (1-2 Marks): Provides a vague or general conclusion or judgement in relation to the question. Makes simple evaluative comment(s) with no development and little supporting evidence.

Indicative content:
AO1 Knowledge and understanding and AO2 Analysis (max 8 marks)
The advantages and disadvantages of using a maximum pricing policy may include:
• If the price is set below the equilibrium price and quantity, then it will make e.g., rice more affordable.
• This will help low-income consumers and improve their living standards and improve their health BUT
• It will create a shortage and informal markets may arise.
• Governments may have to increase their spending to enforce the legislation.
• Rich and poor households gain equally.
Transfer payments may include:
• Unemployment benefits
• Pensions
• Food vouchers etc.
The advantages and disadvantages include:
• They can be targeted at those most in need.
• They can be targeted at the purchase of basic foodstuffs BUT
• May be costly and have an opportunity cost.
• The policies may not be used for their intended purposes (even vouchers may be sold in illegal markets)
A one-sided response can only gain a maximum of Level 2.

AO3 Evaluation (max 4 marks)
• Consideration of the relative effectiveness of a maximum pricing policy compared with the use of transfer payments.
• Consider the impact on low-income consumers of basic foodstuffs
• In conclusion, which policy is most likely to be effective in helping low-income consumers.
A one-sided response cannot gain any marks for evaluation.
responses.studentAnswer: Maximum price is a price ceiling imposed by government on essential items which prevents producers from charging a price below the equilibrium price in the market which prevents producers from charging a price above it. Transfer payments are financial support provided by government to people with low-income to help them meet basic necessities of life in order to prevent income inequality in the society. A policy of fixing the maximum price for an essential food item will help people on low-income afford basic necessities such as rice. This will help protect welfare of people on low-incomes as the maximum price prevents the price of rice from rising which would burden the people on low-incomes. However, maximum price imposed will lead to shortages in the market for rice as the quantity demanded will exceed the quantitied supplied because profit driven firms are not willing to increase supply at a low price because it is not profitable. This causes people to obtain basic necessities on a first come, first serve basis due to the shortage. Furthermore, there will also be emergence of black markets as firms might hide stocks to sell it at a lower price illegally in order to make profit. This would harm people on low-incomes as they would have no choice but to buy the goods at a higher price. On the other hand, transfer payments to low-income households can help increase their purchasing power. This will enable them to afford a whole range of goods and services in the market and not only basic essentials like rice. This can help people on low-income to increase their standard of living as they would be able to afford all basic necessities of life like food and clothes plus other goods and services which can help enhance the quality of their life. However, providing transfer payments to every individual on low-income can be very costly for the government. Governments will face an opportunity cost as funds used to provide transfer payments could have been reallocated to improve healthcare and education which is also vital to the lives of everyone in the society. Furthermore, transfer payments might lead to over dependency among low-income households in the long run as people might lose the motivation to find a better paying job because they believe the government will provide them continuous financial aid. Whether a policy of fixing the maximum price for an essential food is likely to be more effective than transfer payments for low income households depends on the ability of the government to implement an effective rationing system by providing coupons to ensure everyone basic necessities are equally distributed to everyone in the society to overcome the shortage. Besides that it also depends on whether the government is able to control the emergence of black markets by imposing strict regulations and heavy fines on producers who engage in such malpractices. In addition, whether transfer payment will be better than maximum prices would depend on the government's budget position. If the government has high national debts and is running a budget deficit then it will be difficult for government to provide sufficient transfer payments to help low-income households nation wide. In conclusion, transfer payments to low-income households would be more effective compared to maximum prices due to the unintended consequences of maximum price which can lead to a greater strain on the government budget due to the additional measures which need to be taken to ensure its effectiveness.

Indicative content:
AO1 Knowledge and understanding and AO2 Analysis (max 8 marks)
The advantages and disadvantages of using a maximum pricing policy may include:
• If the price is set below the equilibrium price and quantity, then it will make e.g., rice more affordable.

• It will create a shortage and informal markets may arise.
• Governments may have to increase their spending to enforce the legislation.
• Rich and poor households gain equally.
Transfer payments may include:
• Unemployment benefits
• Pensions

The advantages and disadvantages include:
• They can be targeted at those most in need.
• They can be targeted at the purchase of basic foodstuffs BUT
• May be costly and have an opportunity cost.
• The policies may not be used for their intended purposes (even vouchers may be sold in illegal markets)
A one-sided response can only gain a maximum of Level 2.

AO3 Evaluation (max 4 marks)

• Consider the impact on low-income consumers of basic foodstuffs
• In conclusion, which policy is most likely to be effective in helping low-income consumers.
A one-sided response cannot gain any marks for evaluation.
responses.studentAnswer: A maximum price is price ceiling imposed on a gd, that doesn't allow its price to exceed the price ceiling, and is used to make certain goods more affordable for people. The benefit of a maximum price is that essential food such as rice can become for affordable for poorer households, and can reduce their opportunity cost when purchasing them, overall increasing their standards of living. Potential disadvantages of maximum prices can be that there will be an excess demand and a shortage of the good, leading to long waiting lists / queues and a rise in black markets in providing the goods, to which they may charge high prices. A maximum price may also disincentivise firms to produce goods of higher quality, since the prices cannot go beyond a limit, restricting the maximum profit potential they can earn. On the other hand, the policy of making transfer payments to low income households. A transfer payments are money sent to poorer people by the government to help them afford essential gds and increase standard of living. Benefits are that with this extra money, they are able to buy more gds, as well as afford the bare neccessities, improving their standards of living. Transfer payments also has more of a direct impact, leaving them with a higher budget to spend on more things, increasing spending and potentially investment, increasing AD and may lead to economic growth. The drawbacks are that because they are receiving even more money from the government, they may be disincentivised to work, and can increase unemployment rates, leading to slow economic growth and high crime rates, such as stealing. Not only that, transfer payments are expensive and eats a lot into the government budget, and with high unemployment rates, government may not get tax revenue and resort to borrowing from other countries to fund this, increasing debt. The benefits of maximum price only is effective if it is implemented below the price equilibrium of the gd originally, as well as how low the maximum price is. Government may also need to intervene by providing subsidies to firms producing rice, so that they are still incentivised to work, as they have less burden. On the other hand, transfer payments are only effective in the short run, as in the long run, it may be too costly for the government, as well as external factors such as inflation, which can lower the purchasing power of the transfer payments. In conclusion, they are both effective, but maximum prices may be more effective as it has a more controllable disadvantages, whereas transfer payments may cause a large burden on the government in the long run (debt, etc).

responses.studentAnswer: Maximum price and transfer payment were both essential policy for government keep essential necessary food to low income households. Maximum price was only valid when it's set below the equilibrium price and also called price ceiling. Maximum price could ensure a low price which could make those low-income households affordable. Ensure the basic needs to them. However, maximum price exist undesirable effect since it may lead to shortage. QD > QS since the price was very cheap. It could causing waiting problem and the low quality of products as people's demand was very high for neglect this. Transfer payment was those benefit to low income and disable person without introduce of factors of production. For example: pensions, social welfare and food subsidy provision. Transfer payments could reduce the income inequality for provide the unemployment benefits to those low income house. Social holds could rouse the living standard of whole economy. But it exist opportunity cost also. Transfer payment cost could reduce the spending of government to other education or healthca. For both maximum price and transfer payment could be help low on food income households. It depends on the finanial state of government. If the budget was enough, government may use transfer payments policy to providing the welfare. However, it may let households dis-incentive to work since they do nothing and get the welfare. The maximum price for essential food could also happened with blackmarket problem. However government could set the price freely of essential food. In conclusion, maximum price for an essential food may be more effective than policy of making transfer payments since it would cause the disincentive problems.

responses.questionText: Assess whether monetary policy is the only way to control a high rate of inflation.
responses.markScheme:
Table A: AO1 Knowledge and understanding and AO2 Analysis
Level 3 (6-8 Marks): A detailed knowledge and understanding of relevant economic concepts is included, using relevant explanations. Explanations are supported by examples, where appropriate. The response clearly addresses the requirements of the question and explains economic issues, and fully develops these explanations. Analysis is developed and detailed and makes accurate and relevant use of economic concepts and theories.
Level 2 (3-5 Marks): Knowledge and understanding of some relevant economic concepts is included, using explanations and examples that are limited, over-generalised or contain inaccuracies. The response addresses the general theme of the question and the relevant economic issues, with limited development. Analysis is generally accurate with some development but little detail.
Level 1 (1-2 Marks): A small number of relevant knowledge points are included and the response is limited by significant errors or omissions. The response has little relevance to the question. Analysis where provided is largely descriptive.

Table B: AO3 Evaluation
Level 2 (3-4 Marks): Provides a justified conclusion or judgement that addresses the specific requirements of the question. Makes developed, reasoned and well-supported evaluative comment(s).
Level 1 (1-2 Marks): Provides a vague or general conclusion or judgement in relation to the question. Makes simple evaluative comment(s) with no development and little supporting evidence.

Indicative content:
AO1 Knowledge and understanding and AO2 Analysis (max 8 marks)
Explain the advantages and disadvantages of using monetary policy to control high rates of inflation to analyse its effectiveness. Points to consider may include:
• The use of interest rates to dampen AD.
• Reductions in money supply to dampen AD.
• The use of credit regulations to influence bank lending BUT
• There is also a time lag between implementing interest rate policy and it taking effect.
• Other factors other than interest rates may affect consumer spending and offset the impact of a rise in interest rates e.g., consumer confidence, expectations etc.
Monetary policy should be compared with the advantages and disadvantages of at least one other appropriate policy e.g., supply side and/or fiscal policy
A one-sided response can only gain a maximum of Level 2.

AO3 Evaluation (max 4 marks)
• Consideration of the relative effectiveness monetary policy to control high rates of inflation compared with at least one other appropriate policy.
• Although it is not necessary within the analysis / question, reference may be made to the relative effectiveness of these policies in controlling the different types of inflation.
• A justified conclusion showing which policy is most likely to be effective in controlling high rates of inflation.
A one-sided response cannot gain any marks for evaluation.
responses.studentAnswer: Monetary policy is government use money supply, interest rate, exchange rate, credit regulation to influence the AD. Fiscal policy is government use tax and government spending to influence AD, supply-side policy is government improving the produce and labour market to increase AS. The contractionary monetary policy is aim to decrease AD. If government decrease the money supply, the money flow in the economy decrease, less consumption and investment, AD decrease, inflation may decrease. Also, if government increase the interest rate, the people will returns more from saving, so saving increase, comsuption decrease, and the cost of borrowing increase, less firms investment, the AD will decrease, inflation decrease. However, the time lag for interest rate change is long. So the time period for the policy affect is long. On the other hand, the contractionary fiscal policy may also can reduce inflation. If governmat increase the tax on the direct tax. The disopsable income decrease, less consumption, the AD will decrease, inflation decrease. However, the tax on corporation tax may decrease the incentive for supply, the AS may decrease, there may have an inflation. Lastly, the supply-side policy may also can reduce inflation, as government increase the spending on education, training, more innovation and technology, the long run AS will increase. The inflation rate will decrease. However, if the economy's government do not have enough budget, the supply-side policy cannot be exerted, because it is costly. In conclusion, monetary policy can control the high rate of inflation, but supply-side policy, fiscal policy also can reduce the inflation. So, the use of policy depends on the government budget and the causes of inflation, if inflation is caused by too much AD, use contractionary fiscal and monetary policy. If inflation is caused by lack of AS, use supply-side policy.

responses.studentAnswer: Monetary policy was use through change mone supply, interese rate and the credit regulation to to change aggregate demand. The first faCtor, For control a high rate of inflation. Government may control money supply, which could reduce the consumption of whole economy and decrease AD. It's use the contractioney monetary policy to reduce inflation. Talking about interest rate, government may increase interest rate for encourage people saving in the bank. less consumption of luxuey good. With the lower interest rate. Exchange rate would higher also become higher causing the export relartively cheaper than import and reduce the net export revenue for lower the aggregate demand. The credit requlation could be inreased by government to make it difficlut loaning money. Reducing consumption of car and house. lower the AD also. However, monetary policy would not be the only way to control a high rate of inflation since it only control one type of inflation - demand pull. With the solution of supply-side lost push inflation. Government may use supply-side policy to control the problem of decrease of AS. Supply-side poilcy could act on lower the cost of pruduction. With the lower exchange rate for cheaper import price. cheaper raw material price. For reducing the income tax could also let workers be more incentive to work, and increase the labour productivity, which could increase the total aggregate supply. Improve education and innovate new technology could also increased the aggregat supply by increase the quality of labour and capital. All of them could control the cost-push inflation by increase the total AS. So monetary policy was not the only way. The undesirable factor of supply-side policy may be it's too time-consuming. Technology and education could all be long run solution for control the rate of inflation. Monetary policy also exist time lag problem as change the interest rate could not be a immediate action. Also, it may lose consumption as people are saving. AD decrease might also cause recession. In conclusion, monetary policy is not the only way to control a high rate of inflation as it could not solve the cost-push inflation. For supply side policy, it could solve all help of it if government want to control it at short run, monetary policy would be effective. In the other hand, for long run solution, supply side would be best choice.

responses.studentAnswer: Monetary policy is a government macroeconomic intervention policy to increase the money supply in an economy. This can be achieved through the change in interest rates by implying or the rebuying of treasury bills from conventional banks. Inflation is the sustained rise in the general price levels of goods. A contractionary monetary policy is able to control a high rate of inflation as it increases the interest rates. This will decrease the cost of borrowing and incentivise spending. This will cause a higher level of investment as consumers or firms have a higher purchasing power. This will cause an inclease in aggregate demand which may result in demand-pull inflation in the short-run. However, investments in more efficient production methods such as automation or technology will inclease aggregate supply in the long run as firms can produce higher quantity and better quality goods with the same amount of inputs. This will allow for the countering of cost-push inflation. However there are also other policies to control rate of inflation. This can be through a contractionary fiscal policy by increasing tax such as income tax or corporate tax. This will have a more immediate effect as it is more efficient in decreasing disposable income of consumers which disincentivises them from spending. This will decrease aggregate demand for goods in an economy and can counter demand-pull inflation. However this may also not be good as it may disincentivise people from working and migrate to other countries where there is lower tax. This is known as brain drain which will decrease productivity levels of firms and increase COP which may result in cost push inflation. Monetary policy may not have a huge time lag and the extent to which it is effective depends on whether it satisfies the Marshall-Lerner condition which is the sum of elasticities of exports and imports must be more than 1 in order to able to see the effects of depreciating the exchange rate through decreasing interest rates. To conclude, monetary policy may be a good solution in the long run to control inflation however fiscal policy may be better in short run.
